paperAnalysis.title: Minstrel: Structural Prompt Generation with Multi-Agents Coordination for Non-AI Experts
paperAnalysis.coreCapability: Automated generation of reusable, structured prompts via coordinated LLM agents (LangGPT/Minstrel)
paperAnalysis.whatItActuallyDoes: User provides a high‑level task description → Minstrel’s multi‑agent system iteratively drafts, reflects, and assembles a hierarchical prompt template → returns a ready‑to‑use structural prompt that can be plugged into any LLM
keyTechnicalInsights: Treats prompts as a programmable language (syntax, modules) to enable reuse and systematic updates
Multi‑agent coordination with reflection improves prompt quality beyond single‑LLM heuristics
realisticLimitations: Quality depends on underlying LLM API; edge‑case tasks may still need manual tweaking
Multi‑agent orchestration adds latency and cost, limiting scalability for high‑volume SaaS use
Evaluation focused on benchmark tasks; real‑world domain coverage is unproven

productConcept.oneLiner: Generate production‑grade LLM prompts from plain English—no prompt‑engineering required.
productConcept.theHook: Enterprises are deploying LLMs faster than they can safely and effectively prompt them; structured prompts cut errors and boost ROI today.

targetUser.persona: Product Manager responsible for AI‑enabled features at a mid‑size SaaS company
targetUser.painPoint: Spends days iterating trial‑and‑error prompts or hires costly prompt engineers to get reliable LLM output
targetUser.currentAlternatives: Ad‑hoc prompt tinkering, consulting firms, or static prompt libraries with limited adaptability

product.coreFeature: Web UI where a user describes the desired outcome; the platform instantly returns a reusable structural prompt generated by Minstrel‑style multi‑agent workflow
v2Features: Domain‑specific prompt libraries (e.g., sales outreach, data extraction)
API endpoint to fetch generated prompts programmatically
product.differentiation: Uses coordinated LLM agents and reflection to produce higher‑quality, modular prompts that can be updated centrally, unlike static templates or single‑LLM generators.

business.pricingModel: $49/user/mo for the core UI + API access; Enterprise tier with custom prompt libraries and SSO at $399/mo
business.goToMarket: Product‑led growth via a free tier (5 prompts/month) and viral loops from shared prompt templates
business.cohortAnalysis: Early adopters are AI‑focused product teams at B2B SaaS firms who have already purchased LLM credits but lack internal prompt expertise.

metrics.whyThisScore: The underlying research already provides a working multi‑agent pipeline; integrating it into a UI and exposing an API is straightforward, leaving budget and time for polishing UX.

top3Risks: Prompt quality variability across domains may lead to churn if expectations aren’t met
Dependence on third‑party LLM pricing (e.g., OpenAI) could erode margins
Multi‑agent orchestration cost and latency could limit real‑time use cases
realityCheck.whyItMightFail: If the generated structural prompts don’t consistently outperform manually crafted ones, customers will revert to in‑house prompt engineers or generic templates, undermining the value proposition.
pivotOptions: Focus on a vertical (e.g., legal document drafting) and build curated prompt libraries with minimal AI orchestration
Turn the technology into a B2B prompt‑as‑a‑service platform for large enterprises that want to embed prompt generation directly into their internal tools

meta.title: Minstrel: Structural Prompt Generation with Multi-Agents Coordination for Non-AI Experts
meta.abstract: LLMs have demonstrated commendable performance across diverse domains. Nevertheless, formulating high-quality prompts to assist them in their work poses a challenge for non-AI experts. Existing research in prompt engineering suggests somewhat scattered optimization principles and designs empirically dependent prompt optimizers. Unfortunately, these endeavors lack a structural design, incurring high learning costs and it is not conducive to the iterative updating of prompts, especially for non-AI experts. Inspired by structured reusable programming languages, we propose LangGPT, a structural prompt design framework. Furthermore, we introduce Minstrel, a multi-generative agent system with reflection to automate the generation of structural prompts. Experiments and the case study illustrate that structural prompts generated by Minstrel or written manually significantly enhance the performance of LLMs. Furthermore, we analyze the ease of use of structural prompts through a user survey in our online community.

ideas.oneLiner: Instantly combine pre‑trained vision models into a single lightweight model for indie game asset creation without any training.
ideas.theHook: The indie game market surged to $2B in 2025, but developers lack resources to fine‑tune or train custom vision models; AutoMerge enables fast, cost‑free model blending.

targetUser.painPoint: Needs a custom image‑style or object‑detection model for sprite generation but cannot afford GPU‑intensive fine‑tuning.
targetUser.currentAlternatives: Fine‑tuning a large pretrained model on cloud GPUs or using multiple separate models at runtime.

ideas.coreTech: AutoMerge’s block‑level segmentation + search‑based selection of merging technique & hyper‑parameters for CV architectures (ResNet, EfficientNet).

product.coreFeature: Web UI where users upload two or three small vision models and receive a merged, trimmed model ready for Unity/Unreal integration in <5 minutes.
product.differentiation: No training needed, sub‑MB model size, automatic hyper‑parameter search guarantees higher accuracy than naive weight averaging.

business.pricingModel: Freemium – 2 free merges per month; $19/mo for unlimited merges + priority compute; enterprise licenses for studios.
business.gtm: Partner with Unity Asset Store, sponsor game jam challenges, targeted ads on Reddit r/gamedev and Discord dev communities.

ideas.whyThisAngle: A quick‑to‑market SaaS that solves a tangible, revenue‑ready need for a growing creator base while showcasing AutoMerge’s core capability in a single domain.

ideas.oneLiner: Merge multiple specialty models into one ultra‑compact AI for edge‑deployed agricultural drones.
ideas.theHook: Regulatory pressure in 2025 demands sub‑5 W AI on drones; AutoMerge can collapse several large models into a single efficient model, unlocking multi‑task capability on a single board.

targetUser.persona: Agriculture drone operator / field data engineer
targetUser.painPoint: Runs separate models for crop disease detection, canopy segmentation, and obstacle avoidance, hitting compute & battery limits.
targetUser.currentAlternatives: Multiple GPU‑heavy models on the drone or off‑loading inference to the cloud (high latency, connectivity issues).

ideas.coreTech: AutoMerge’s heterogeneous block segmentation applied to CNN (for vision) + LSTM (for trajectory) + small transformer heads, with automated hyper‑parameter tuning for edge constraints.

product.coreFeature: Desktop CLI that ingests a set of pre‑trained models and outputs a single ONNX model fitting a <10 MB memory budget and <10 ms inference on NVIDIA Jetson Nano.
product.differentiation: Guarantees <3 % accuracy loss vs. separate models while cutting total FLOPs by 40 % using AutoMerge’s search‑driven technique selection.

business.pricingModel: License per drone unit – $299 for one‑off merge, $99/month for OTA re‑merge updates; volume discounts for fleets.
business.gtm: Direct sales to precision‑ag companies, demos at AgriTech conferences, integration partners like DJI and Parrot.

ideas.whyThisAngle: A niche vertical with clear hardware constraints makes the AutoMerge advantage (heterogeneous block handling) uniquely valuable and defensible.

ideas.oneLiner: API‑first model‑merging platform that auto‑combines any compatible models, letting developers build bespoke AI without retraining.
ideas.theHook: As model marketplaces explode, developers struggle to compose models; AutoMerge supplies a universal, search‑based merging service that works across vision, NLP, and RL architectures.

targetUser.painPoint: Wants to create a hybrid model (e.g., sentiment‑aware image captioning) but lacks compute for joint training.
targetUser.currentAlternatives: Manual weight averaging, ensemble inference, or costly fine‑tuning on cloud GPUs.

ideas.coreTech: AutoMerge’s automated block partitioning + combinatorial search across five merging algorithms, exposed via REST & SDKs for PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX.

product.coreFeature: Upload up to 5 models; receive a merged model plus a reproducible JSON plan detailing chosen techniques & hyper‑parameters.
product.differentiation: One‑click merging across heterogeneous architectures with proven performance bounds; auto‑generated documentation for downstream deployment.

business.pricingModel: Pay‑as‑you‑go compute credits ($0.10 per merge minute) + subscription tier ($29/mo) for priority queue and model storage.
business.gtm: Integration with Hugging Face Hub, developer evangelism on GitHub, tutorials on Kaggle and Stack Overflow.

ideas.whyThisAngle: By turning AutoMerge into a service, we create a reusable layer for countless downstream products, maximizing network effects while staying technology‑centric.

ideas.oneLiner: Unify heterogeneous anomaly‑detection models into a single high‑speed detector for SOC analysts using search‑based model merging.
ideas.theHook: Post‑2025 cyber‑attack surge demands real‑time detection on limited SIEM hardware; merging eliminates ensemble latency without sacrificing specialty model knowledge.

targetUser.persona: SOC (Security Operations Center) analyst in mid‑size firms
targetUser.painPoint: Maintains separate models for network traffic, host logs, and user behavior; ensemble scoring causes >2 s delay, missing fast attacks.
targetUser.currentAlternatives: Running multiple models in parallel on beefy servers or coarse‑grained rule‑based systems.

ideas.coreTech: AutoMerge’s heterogeneous block‑wise merging across Transformer‑based log models, CNN‑based packet classifiers, and Graph‑Neural‑Network user graphs, with automated hyper‑parameter search to preserve detection thresholds.

product.coreFeature: CLI tool that consumes three pre‑trained detection models and outputs a single model <30 % of original memory, delivering <200 ms inference on a standard server.
product.differentiation: Retains >95 % of each source model’s recall while cutting compute by 60 %—a result unattainable by simple averaging.

business.pricingModel: Annual license per monitored environment ($2,500) + optional consulting for model selection.
business.gtm: Direct outreach to MSSPs, webinars at Black Hat/RSAC, case studies with open‑source SIEMs like Elastic Stack.

ideas.whyThisAngle: Security teams undervalue model merging; offering a fast, low‑cost solution meets an urgent need while few competitors focus on this niche.

meta.title: AutoMerge: Search-Based Model Merging Framework for Effective Model Reuse
meta.abstract: Software reuse has long been recognized as a critical and widely studied topic in software engineering, offering substantial benefits in reducing development costs, improving software quality, and enhancing operational efficiency. This paradigm extends into deep learning through model reuse. Recently, model merging has emerged in the domain of large language models (LLMs) as a training-free approach that takes multiple task-specific models with the same architecture as source models and merges them without retraining, enhancing model reuse within LLMs. However, no prior work has systematically investigated whether such an approach can be effectively applied to other deep learning models with different architectures across domains. To bridge this gap, we present the first systematic study that evaluates five model merging techniques on three distinct model architectures across three domains: LLMs, image classification, and autonomous driving. Our findings reveal that directly applying existing model merging techniques leads to highly inconsistent results and falls notably short of their success within LLMs. Moreover, a single model merging technique often fails to handle the heterogeneous structural properties within a model, limiting its applicability to different model architectures across domains. Furthermore, the effectiveness of model merging techniques is highly sensitive to hyperparameter configurations, thereby constraining their potential for broader adoption. Inspired by these insights, we propose AutoMerge, a novel search-based model merging framework that first segments complex models into multiple heterogeneous blocks and then systematically explores the merging space to identify the merging technique and its hyperparameter configuration.

ideas.oneLiner: A VS Code plug‑in that instantly generates, completes, and debugs snippets for freelance front‑end developers.
ideas.theHook: LLMs now run cheaper on inference‑optimized GPUs and the market of 1M+ independent web developers is hungry for instant productivity boosts.

targetUser.persona: Freelance front‑end developer (e.g., building React/Next.js sites for small businesses)
targetUser.painPoint: Spends 30‑40% of time searching StackOverflow or copy‑pasting boilerplate; debugging browser errors is time‑consuming.
targetUser.currentAlternatives: Manual search, generic code snippet libraries, occasional use of Copilot (requires subscription).

coreTech.capability: LLM‑driven code generation & bug‑fix suggestion tuned on front‑end stacks (React, CSS, TypeScript).
coreTech.implementation:
VS Code extension written in TypeScript that streams the current file + selected region to a backend service. Backend runs an OpenAI‑compatible fine‑tuned Llama 2‑7B model on NVIDIA T4 GPUs, using a prompt template: <file‑context> + "Fix this bug:" + <selection>. Results are streamed back via WebSocket and inserted inline. Uses a local cache to store recent prompts for latency <500 ms.
Tech stack: Node.js API (Fastify), LangChain for prompt orchestration, Faiss vector store for code‑context retrieval, Docker‑compose deployment on Azure Container Instances.
coreTech.limitations: Hallucinated code may compile but break logic; limited context window (~8 k tokens) for large files; proprietary code sent to cloud raises IP concerns.

product.coreFeature: One‑click ‘Generate/Debug’ button that returns a ready‑to‑insert snippet and a short explanation.
v2Features: Workspace‑wide pattern library learned from a developer’s own repos.
Automated unit‑test generation for the suggested snippet.
Privacy‑first self‑hosted mode for enterprise freelancers.
product.differentiation: Focused on front‑end tech, lightweight prompt engineering, and instant inline insertion—far cheaper than full‑stack copilots.

business.pricingModel: Freemium: 50 generations/month free, then $15/mo for unlimited plus priority support.
business.gtm: Launch on Reddit r/webdev, partner with popular front‑end tutorial sites for coupon codes, and list on VS Marketplace with a 4‑star launch badge.
business.cohort: Top‑10% of freelancers who earn >$5k/month and already use VS Code extensions.

topRisks: LLM hallucinations lead to broken UI.
API cost spikes if usage grows faster than price tuning.
realityCheck.whyItMightFail: If developers distrust auto‑generated code, adoption stalls; also high competition from GitHub Copilot’s brand.
pivotOptions: Shift to a SaaS code‑review bot for small agencies.
Offer the engine as a private API for agency tooling.

ideas.whyThisAngle: A narrow, high‑velocity segment (freelance front‑end devs) lets us ship fast, monetize quickly, and prove the LLM‑code pipeline before tackling larger IDE markets.

ideas.oneLiner: Domain‑tuned LLM that drafts, validates, and documents R scripts for biostatisticians running clinical trials.
ideas.theHook: Regulatory deadlines are tightening (FDA 2026 guidance) and biostatisticians need reproducible, compliant code faster than ever.

targetUser.persona: Senior biostatistician at a mid‑size pharma contract research organization (CRO)
targetUser.painPoint: Writing repetitive data‑cleaning and analysis scripts takes weeks; audit trails must be precise, and errors trigger costly re‑runs.
targetUser.currentAlternatives: Manual R scripting, internal code libraries, occasional hire of junior analysts.

coreTech.capability: LLM fine‑tuned on a curated corpus of FDA‑approved trial analysis scripts, clinical guidelines, and CRAN packages (survival, lme4).
coreTech.implementation: Create a private dataset of 10 k anonymized R scripts from past trials (secured via data‑use agreements). Fine‑tune a Meta‑Llama‑13B model on this dataset using LoRA adapters. Deploy as a secure gRPC service behind a HIPAA‑compliant VPC. The front‑end is a Shiny web app where users upload a trial protocol (PDF) → OCR → semantic chunking → prompt to LLM: "Generate data‑cleaning script for variables X, Y, Z". Output includes inline comments and a reproducibility report (checksum, package versions).
coreTech.limitations: Regulatory compliance requires rigorous validation; LLM may generate statistically invalid models; limited ability to interpret proprietary trial designs without proper prompt engineering.

product.coreFeature: One‑click generation of a fully commented R script aligned to a supplied protocol section.
v2Features: Automated unit‑test suite comparing generated output to a gold‑standard dataset.
product.differentiation: Clinical‑domain fine‑tuning + built‑in regulatory compliance checklist—nothing else offers a dedicated R‑trial assistant.

business.pricingModel: Enterprise license $3 k/mo per user, includes 200 script generations and compliance audit logs.
business.gtm: Direct sales to CROs via conferences (SISA, ISPOR), pilot projects with 2‑3 CROs offering free first 20 scripts, publish case studies on time‑to‑analysis reduction.
business.cohort: Biostatisticians handling >5 concurrent Phase II/III trials.

topRisks: Regulatory bodies may reject AI‑generated analyses.
Data privacy constraints on using real trial data for model training.
realityCheck.whyItMightFail: If the LLM fails to meet statistical rigor, firms will revert to manual coding despite speed promises.
pivotOptions: Turn into a generic R‑analysis assistant for academic researchers.
License the fine‑tuned model to existing statistical platforms (e.g., SAS).

ideas.whyThisAngle: A tightly regulated niche limits competition and justifies premium pricing; the domain‑specific LLM creates a defensible moat early on.

ideas.oneLiner: API‑first platform that auto‑generates maintainable documentation for any codebase using LLM‑driven retrieval‑augmented generation.
ideas.theHook: Open‑source projects are exploding (2 M+ repos on GitHub 2025) but documentation lags, causing contributor friction and security risks.

targetUser.persona: Open‑source library maintainer (core maintainer of a Python package with >5 k stars)
targetUser.painPoint: Spends >30 % of time writing/updating READMEs, API docs, and changelogs; inconsistent docs deter contributors.
targetUser.currentAlternatives: Manual Sphinx docs, MkDocs, community‑driven docs, occasional use of ChatGPT for snippets.

coreTech.capability: LLM‑powered documentation generation with code‑context retrieval (RAG) and multi‑modal output (Markdown, Jupyter, OpenAPI).
coreTech.implementation: Platform built on FastAPI exposing `/generate-doc` endpoint. When a repo URL is posted, a background worker clones the repo, runs a language‑agnostic parser (tree‑sitter) to index symbols, and stores embeddings in a Pinecone vector DB. Prompt is built: "Generate a concise markdown API doc for function X, include example usage, and note deprecation warnings". Uses a GPT‑4‑turbo model with system messages enforcing style guide. Returns a PR‑ready markdown diff. SDKs for Python/JS allow CI integration (GitHub Actions).
coreTech.limitations: LLM may hallucinate parameters not present; indexing large monorepos can be slow; depends on public repo visibility for embeddings.

product.coreFeature: One‑click PR that adds or refreshes module‑level documentation directly on GitHub.
v2Features: auto‑generated changelog from commit messages + LLM summarization.
multi‑language support (Python, Go, Rust) via language‑specific parsers.
product.differentiation: Platform‑wide retrieval of whole repo context ensures coherence across files; SDK enables any CI pipeline to call it, unlike point‑solutions.

business.pricingModel: Tiered SaaS: Free 5‑doc/month for public repos, Pro $49/mo for private repos + unlimited docs, Enterprise custom.
business.gtm: Partner with GitHub Marketplace, sponsor open‑source conferences, run a “Doc‑Sprint” hackathon where participants auto‑doc their projects using the API.
business.cohort: Maintainers of libraries with >1 k stars who have <2 contributors per month.

topRisks: GitHub rate limits on cloning large repos.
Maintainers may reject auto‑generated PRs as noisy.
realityCheck.whyItMightFail: If the generated docs are inaccurate, trust erodes quickly; also high churn in open‑source tooling landscape.
pivotOptions: Offer the engine as an internal docs generator for enterprises.
License the indexing + RAG pipeline to code‑review tools.

ideas.whyThisAngle: A platform creates network effects (more repos → richer embeddings → better outputs) and unlocks B2B licensing later while addressing an acute, measurable pain point.

ideas.oneLiner: LLM‑driven security auditor that scans legacy COBOL mainframe codebases for vulnerabilities and auto‑generates remediation patches.
ideas.theHook: Banks and insurers still run 30% of transaction processing on COBOL; regulators are mandating modern security standards (2026 PCI DSS v5) but lack tools for legacy code.

targetUser.persona: Mainframe maintenance engineer at a regional bank (30–50 M daily transactions).
targetUser.painPoint: Finding security flaws in millions of lines of COBOL is manual, error‑prone, and costly; auditors frequently flag undocumented risky patterns.
targetUser.currentAlternatives: Manual code reviews, proprietary static analysis tools with high false‑positive rates, third‑party consultancy audits.

coreTech.capability: LLM fine‑tuned on a corpus of de‑identified COBOL code annotated with CWE‑type vulnerabilities and remediation snippets.
coreTech.implementation: Collect 5 k open‑source COBOL examples plus 1 k annotated security patches from public breach disclosures (scrubbed). Fine‑tune a 7B Llama model using PEFT (LoRA) for vulnerability classification and patch generation. Deploy as an on‑prem Docker container (no external API) to satisfy data‑sovereignty constraints. The tool integrates with IBM Z Open Developer Kit: it parses source files via ANTLR grammar, feeds each function to the model with prompt: "Identify any security issue and propose a safe rewrite." Outputs a SARIF report and optional patch diff. Orchestrated with a lightweight Rust CLI wrapper for performance.
coreTech.limitations: COBOL parsing edge‑cases, potential false‑negatives, and the need for rigorous validation before production deployment.

product.coreFeature: Command‑line scanner that returns a SARIF report with line‑by‑line vulnerability tags and one‑click patch suggestions.
v2Features: Integration with z/OS Change Management to auto‑apply approved patches.
Compliance dashboard mapping findings to PCI/Dodd‑Frank controls.
Continuous monitoring daemon that re‑scans on code check‑in.
product.differentiation: First AI product that *writes* secure COBOL patches; most tools only flag issues. On‑prem deployment respects strict banking data policies.

business.pricingModel: Per‑core‑license $8 k/yr (includes unlimited scans, support, and quarterly model updates).
business.gtm: Leverage existing relationships of mainframe consultancies, attend IBM Z forums, offer a free pilot on a non‑production branch.
business.cohort: Security‑focused mainframe teams at banks/insurance firms with >10 M $/yr IT budgets.

topRisks: Regulatory auditors may reject AI‑generated patches without human sign‑off.
Scarcity of high‑quality annotated COBOL security data limits model performance.
realityCheck.whyItMightFail: If false positives overwhelm teams, adoption stalls; also legacy teams may be resistant to any AI intervention.
pivotOptions: Shift to a pure vulnerability detection SaaS (no auto‑patch).
Apply the same pipeline to other legacy languages (Fortran, PL/SQL).

ideas.whyThisAngle: Most LLM‑code startups ignore legacy ecosystems; targeting COBOL exploits a massive, cash‑rich market with almost no competition, turning a perceived downside (old tech) into a moat.

meta.title: From Idea to Implementation: Evaluating the Influence of Large Language Models in Software Development -- An Opinion Paper
meta.abstract: The introduction of transformer architecture was a turning point in Natural Language Processing (NLP). Models based on the transformer architecture such as Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) and Generative Pre-Trained Transformer (GPT) have gained widespread popularity in various applications such as software development and education. The availability of Large Language Models (LLMs) such as ChatGPT and Bard to the general public has showcased the tremendous potential of these models and encouraged their integration into various domains such as software development for tasks such as code generation, debugging, and documentation generation. In this study, opinions from 11 experts regarding their experience with LLMs for software development have been gathered and analysed to draw insights that can guide successful and responsible integration. The overall opinion of the experts is positive, with the experts identifying advantages such as increase in productivity and reduced coding time. Potential concerns and challenges such as risk of over-dependence and ethical considerations have also been highlighted.

data.oneLiner: AI research partner that generates novel, grant‑ready research hypotheses in seconds.
data.theHook: The flood of AI‑augmented writing tools has made generic text generation cheap, but only a handful of models are trained to *invent* next‑step science—DeepInnovator’s next‑idea prediction unlocks a truly innovative assistant just as funding agencies tighten criteria for novelty.

targetUser.persona: Early‑career academic (PhD student or postdoc) preparing grant proposals or paper outlines
targetUser.painPoint: Spending weeks scouring literature and brainstorming to find a genuinely novel hypothesis that will impress reviewers
targetUser.currentAlternatives: Manual literature reviews, generic LLMs (ChatGPT, Claude), and ad‑hoc brainstorming sessions

data.coreTech: DeepInnovator’s “Next Idea Prediction” training paradigm and the open‑source 14B model fine‑tuned on structured research knowledge extraction

product.coreFeature: Web UI where users input a research context and receive a ranked list of plausible, novel next‑step ideas with brief justification and suggested experiments
product.differentiation: Ideas are generated by a model explicitly trained to predict *innovative* next steps, not just recite existing knowledge; includes an automatic plausibility filter that scores novelty vs. feasibility

business.pricingModel: Tiered SaaS: Free tier – 3 ideas per month; Pro tier – $29/user / month for unlimited ideas & API access; Enterprise – custom pricing with domain‑specific fine‑tuning
business.gtm: Launch a freemium beta through university labs and research incubators, partner with graduate‑school workshops, run targeted LinkedIn ads to PhD candidates, and publish case studies showing grant‑success uplift

suggestions.oneLiner: An AI-powered literature review assistant that automatically extracts, structures, and maps existing research to surface hidden gaps and generate novel research directions for academic labs.
suggestions.angle: Vertical for academic research groups and university labs, focusing on systematic literature mining and hypothesis generation.

suggestions.oneLiner: A platform that uses trained LLMs to create grant‑ready, high‑impact research proposals tailored to biotech and deep‑tech venture pipelines, accelerating deal flow for investors.
suggestions.angle: B‑to‑B service for venture capital firms and corporate R&D, turning market trends into concrete, fundable science projects.

suggestions.oneLiner: An interactive learning tool for graduate students that simulates a research partner, guiding them through idea iteration cycles, critique, and refinement to build strong thesis proposals.
suggestions.angle: Educational SaaS targeting higher‑education students and instructors, turning the research‑agent model into a teaching assistant.

meta.title: DeepInnovator: Triggering the Innovative Capabilities of LLMs
meta.abstract: The application of Large Language Models (LLMs) in accelerating scientific discovery has garnered increasing attention, with a key focus on constructing research agents endowed with innovative capability, i.e., the ability to autonomously generate novel and significant research ideas. Existing approaches predominantly rely on sophisticated prompt engineering and lack a systematic training paradigm. To address this, we propose DeepInnovator, a training framework designed to trigger the innovative capability of LLMs. Our approach comprises two core components. (1) ``Standing on the shoulders of giants''. We construct an automated data extraction pipeline to extract and organize structured research knowledge from a vast corpus of unlabeled scientific literature. (2) ``Conjectures and refutations''. We introduce a ``Next Idea Prediction'' training paradigm, which models the generation of research ideas as an iterative process of continuously predicting, evaluating, and refining plausible and novel next idea. Both automatic and expert evaluations demonstrate that our DeepInnovator-14B significantly outperforms untrained baselines, achieving win rates of 80.53\%-93.81\%, and attains performance comparable to that of current leading LLMs. This work provides a scalable training pathway toward building research agents with genuine, originative innovative capability, and will open-source the dataset to foster community advancement. Source code and data are available at: https://github.com/HKUDS/DeepInnovator.
